articles.title: PLEASE STOP THE AI CONFIDENCE THEATER
articles.summary: AI hype creates anxiety and erodes trust. Real progress comes from honest expectations, measurable outcomes, and the sustained work required to make AI reliable.

articles.title: GREAT PRODUCTS, BAD COMPANIES
articles.summary: Great products need strong governance. Without it, short-term financial interests can undermine a company's founders, culture, and mission.

articles.title: EXECUTION PROBLEMS ARE OFTEN WORKFLOW PROBLEMS
articles.summary: Execution improves when workflows do not depend on perfect collaboration. Separate learning, debate, and decisions so progress can continue despite unreliable dependencies.

articles.title: WHERE DOES PRODUCT GO WRONG IN PE-BACKED FIRMS? (57 MINUTE VIDEO)
HOW PRODUCT TEAMS CAN DEVELOP THEIR PRODUCT VISION COLLABORATIVELY
articles.summary: A strong product vision connects the team to a meaningful change for users and guides difficult decisions. It should be collaborative, grounded in customer insight, and tested through real scenarios.

articles.title: HOW TO BE A SECURITY PERSON WHO DOESN'T SUCK AT PRODUCT
articles.summary: AI is turning security professionals from gatekeepers into builders. Success will require engineering skill, product judgment, and security experiences that developers want to use.

articles.title: WE LIKE ANTHROPIC MORE THAN OPENAI. THE TROLLEY PROBLEM EXPLAINS WHY. (4 MINUTE VIDEO)
articles.summary: Visible deliberation builds trust. When making a difficult decision, explain the tradeoffs and show that you considered its impact carefully.

articles.title: MOST AI WORK CAN WAIT
articles.summary: Build AI systems around intelligent routing, not a single model. Send each task to the cheapest reliable option and process nonurgent work asynchronously.

articles.title: THE FUNDAMENTAL COGNITIVE BIAS OF PRODUCT BUILDERS
articles.summary: Many product builders blame weak marketing for failure while crediting competitors' success to distribution, all while assuming their own product is better.

articles.title: WHEN SHOULD YOU KNOW THE POINT?
articles.summary: Clear goals can improve decisions, but not every valuable activity needs a precise purpose.
